title: Existing app takeover case study | Bc. Ondřej Halata (halatao.cz)

seo.title: Existing app takeover case study | Bc. Ondřej Halata (halatao.cz)
seo.description: An anonymised case-study scenario showing how an existing app takeover can reduce delivery risk and restore a practical roadmap.

page.title: Existing app takeover case study | Bc. Ondřej Halata (halatao.cz)
page.h1: Case study: existing app takeover and delivery stabilisation
page.description: An anonymised case-study scenario showing how an existing app takeover can reduce delivery risk and restore a practical roadmap.

hero.title: The product was live, but every change felt risky
hero.subtitle: A representative takeover situation after a vendor change or a period without strong technical ownership.

intro: The application was in production, but the team had limited confidence in touching important parts of it. Release flow was fragile and roadmap progress was slowing down.
The buyer did not need a dramatic rewrite. The buyer needed clarity on real risk and a safer way to continue.

sections.title: Starting situation
body: This is a representative anonymised scenario based on real project patterns, not an inflated marketing story.
bullets: too much caution around routine changes

sections.title: How the work was approached
body: The first step was not a maximalist scope. It was understanding risk, priorities, and the right size of the first delivery phase.
bullets: fast orientation in architecture and release flow
stabilising selected parts before broader work

sections.title: What changed
body: The point is not to fake exact numbers. It is to show the kind of change a similar project can create.

faq.question: Does app takeover always require a long audit phase?
faq.answer: No. A focused first phase can often surface the main risks and priorities quickly.

faq.question: Do you need to redesign the whole architecture immediately?
faq.answer: Usually not. For running apps, it is often better to secure the risky parts and improve delivery confidence first.

faq.question: Can this work alongside the client’s internal team?
faq.answer: Yes. That is often the best way to rebuild control over the system quickly.
